name: Beef Allocator API
summary: Beef preserves `new` syntax but makes allocator selection a first-class part of allocation expressions: `scope` for lexical-lifetime objects, `new` for the workspace-selected global allocator, and `new:allocator` for a custom allocator object.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Beef preserves familiar `new` syntax but makes allocator selection a first-class part of allocation expressions: `scope` for lexical-lifetime objects (RAII-style automatic deallocation at scope exit), `new` for the workspace-selected global allocator, and `new:allocator` for a custom allocator object that implements at least `Alloc` and `Free`.

Core types like `String` and `List` expose overridable hooks; the runtime ships a debug allocator with real-time leak checking and hot-compilation support. The model sits between Odin's context-allocator implicit policy and Zig's per-call allocator parameter.

sections.title: Allocation expressions and debug allocator
sections.content:
`scope MyType()` allocates on the local stack frame's scope — the object is destroyed when the scope exits, akin to a stack-allocated C++ value but heap-friendly. `new MyType()` uses the workspace-configured global allocator; `new:myalloc MyType()` routes through `myalloc` (any object implementing the allocator interface). Library types like `List<T>` accept allocator overrides on construction, mirroring the per-data-structure choice that Zig makes mandatory.

The debug allocator with real-time leak checking integrates with Beef's hot-recompile workflow: leaks reported on scope exit during a hot-recompiled run point at the offending allocation site without needing a separate run.
